name: Cambridge (Massachusetts, United States)

colors.symbolism: the outer ring of the city seal and the lettering
the upper half of the seal's interior — sky and learning
the ribbon bearing the Latin name of the city
the tree depicted on the seal

description: A white field with the city seal in the centre — an outer yellow-bordered ring bearing the Latin mottoes "LITERIS ANTIQVIS NOVIS INSTITVTIS DECORA" ("Adorned with classical learning and new institutions") and "CIVICO REGIMINE DONATA A.D. 1646" ("Granted city government A.D. 1646"); the seal's interior is divided blue (above) over white (below), depicting a blue building with a tree, a gold garland above and a red ribbon below inscribed "CANTABRIGIA" (the Latin name of Cambridge); below the seal, in gold, the inscription "CONCITA A.D. 1636" ("Founded A.D. 1636").
history: Cambridge — founded in 1630 as Newtowne and renamed Cambridge in 1638 in honour of the English university — uses a flag centred on its civic seal, which references its incorporation as a town in 1636 and as a city in 1646. The Latin mottoes celebrate the city's role as the home of Harvard College (founded 1636), the oldest institution of higher learning in what became the United States.

features.symbolism: plain white field bearing the seal of the City of Cambridge

features.role: seal of the City of Cambridge
features.symbolism: the seal commemorates Cambridge's foundation in 1630 ("CONCITA A.D. 1636" — recording the town's incorporation), its grant of city government in 1646 ("CIVICO REGIMINE DONATA A.D. 1646"), and its identity as the home of Harvard College and centre of classical and modern learning ("LITERIS ANTIQVIS NOVIS INSTITVTIS DECORA")
